calibration_note: Dataset shows 14 Easy, 5 Medium, 0 Hard. This is an undercalibration by the extraction model. For generation, treat most Easy-tagged questions as Medium. Hard Algebra questions are defined by the blueprint in Component 2C.

coverage_map.coverage_note: Strong concentration in foundational algebraic conditions (inequalities, quadratics, absolute values). Advanced algebraic fractions and radicals underrepresented.

mechanical_signals.steps: Easy = single-rule execution. Medium = execution plus verification.
mechanical_signals.concept_mixing: Easy isolates subtopics. Medium fuses them.
mechanical_signals.qualifier_sensitivity: Medium pivots on logical bounds (always, certainly, for all real values).
mechanical_signals.answer_similarity: Wrong options vary by a single strict/non-strict inequality sign or parameter.

genuine_easy: M2Q42 (sum of absolute values < 0 -> empty set)
should_be_medium: M4Q1 (solve quadratic then filter against linear inequality domain)
M4Q23 (parameter axiom with a≠0 edge case)
recalibration.hardest_in_set: M1Q39 — only true structural multi-step object (nested fractional simplification). Closest bridge to Hard classification.

EASY.description: Single-rule execution. One concept. No domain verification required. Answer is immediate.

MEDIUM.description: Execution followed by verification. Two concepts fused. Qualifier sensitivity active. Requires checking result against original constraint.

HARD_blueprint.description: A parameter controls the number of valid solutions. Algebraically correct intermediate solutions must be rejected because they violate a secondary constraint. Requires simultaneous evaluation of multiple constraint domains.
HARD_blueprint.blueprint_example: For which values of k does x^2 - 4x + k = 0 AND |x-2| <= 1 have exactly one valid real solution? Forces simultaneous discriminant boundary evaluation AND geometric interval filtering — rejecting roots that fall outside the absolute value domain.
HARD_blueprint.key_rule: Hard questions present algebraically correct solutions that must be rejected because they violate a secondary constraint.

signals.answer_similarity: high - options differ by which roots pass the filter

SIGN_ERROR.mechanism: Student mishandles negation during inequality operations — fails to flip inequality sign when dividing by negative.
examples: M1Q27 (solving 36-4c>=0 by dividing by -4, forgetting to flip: yields c>=9 instead of c<=9)

EXTRANEOUS_SOLUTION.mechanism: Student accepts mathematically valid intermediate solution that violates original premise or domain restriction.
examples: M4Q1 (x^2=25 yields ±5, failing to filter -5 against x+4>0 — selects Two instead of One)

SHORTCUT.mechanism: Student applies memorised template while ignoring specific constraints stated in the problem.
examples: M3Q38 (factors as (x-x1)(x-x2) ignoring mandatory leading coefficient a)
M4Q23 (assumes ax=b always has one solution, ignoring a≠0 requirement)

SCOPE.mechanism: Student maps a locally true result to a global domain — conclusion valid for some values treated as valid for all.
examples: M4Q10 (d1 and d2 share roots 1 and 2, student assumes same solution SET — fails to see squaring expands d2 to all R)

ZERO_INFINITY_EDGE_CASE.mechanism: Question pivots on what happens at a boundary value — zero, infinity, or empty set.
examples: M2Q42 and M3Q33 (sum of absolute values < 0 yields empty set)
M1Q3 and M2Q46 (proportional linear systems yield infinite solutions)

FACTORING_SHORTCUT.mechanism: Structural misidentification — student fails to recognise a factorable form and expands messily instead.
examples: M3Q50 (fails to group (x-y-z)(x-z+y) as difference of squares (x-z)^2 - y^2)

error_frequency_map.most_common: Extraneous Solution — accepting intermediate roots without domain verification
error_frequency_map.second: Shortcut — applying general rule to specific case with additional constraint
error_frequency_map.third: Sign Error — inequality direction on negative division
error_frequency_map.fourth: Scope — local truth treated as global

time_pressure_anatomy.verification_burden: Primary time cost. Initial calculation is fast; cognitive shift to verify roots against secondary constraint is where time bleeds. M4Q1 and M1Q27 exemplify this — solving is instant, verification is the test.
time_pressure_anatomy.false_speed_trap: M4Q10 looks like a double-sign-chart inequality problem. Structurally aware student recognises (x-1)^2(x-2)^2 >= 0 is trivially true for all R — solves in 5 seconds instead of 2 minutes.
time_pressure_anatomy.option_elimination_strategy: M2Q3: premise states y<0<z. Option C is literally z>0 — a direct restatement. Students lose 90 seconds on complex algebra instead of scanning options. M2Q11: P(1)=0 instantly flags (x-1) as a factor — no polynomial division needed.

layer1_problem_structure.constraint_layering: Increase complexity by layering constraints without extending computation length. Fuse two subtopics (discriminant + logarithmic domain bounds).
layer1_problem_structure.medium_vs_easy_distinction: M1Q39 harder because nested LCD resolution — single dropped denominator cascades to failure. M2Q11 harder because root maps to functional abstraction P(1)=0 => (x-1)Q(x), not numerical solving.
layer1_problem_structure.hard_structure: Parameter controls the number of valid solutions. Solutions must satisfy a secondary constraint. Algebraically correct solutions that fail the secondary constraint are engineered as distractors.

layer2_cognitive_load.case_splitting: Force explicit case splits: |x-2| = 2x-1 requires two branches, each with extraneous solution filtering.
layer2_cognitive_load.domain_restriction: Require verification of domain restrictions after every solution step.
layer2_cognitive_load.parametric_framing: Use unknown constants a, b, c to strip away the student's ability to verify by testing integers.

model.correct: Logically airtight intersection of all constraints
model.extraneous_trap: Most critical distractor — root derived from intermediate algebra that fails original domain restriction
model.sign_error_trap: Reverses inequality on negative division or flips critical root sign
model.shortcut_trap: Standard formula applied while ignoring edge case in stem
model.scope_trap: Conclusion true for localised subset stated as universally true via qualifier

layer3_answer_engineering.mapping_example: Discriminant parameter c: Correct (c<=9) | Sign Error (c>=9) | Shortcut Arithmetic (c<=6) | Scope Trap (for any real value)
layer3_answer_engineering.elimination_mapping_rule: Every wrong option derives from a specific algebraic error. Options must be algebraically plausible, never numerically random.

figure_scenario_patterns.note: Full dataset review confirms 0 questions contain figures. Bocconi Algebra is overwhelmingly pure symbolic.

framing_dictates_difficulty.abstract_symbolic: Variables or financial parameters without real-world context force purely syntactic manipulation (M2Q28).
framing_dictates_difficulty.explicit_vs_implicit_conditions: 'The equation certainly has' (M2Q27) tests sufficiency — student must prove universal validity, not find one case.
framing_dictates_difficulty.parametric_isolation: Unknown constants a, b, c (M3Q38, M4Q23) strip ability to verify by testing integers — pure algebraic logic required.

necessary_vs_sufficient_confusion.description: The primary Algebra ambiguity mechanism. Students treat a sufficient condition as necessary, or a necessary condition as sufficient. When a question asks 'for which values does X certainly occur', the correct answer requires proving the condition holds universally. Students select answers that make it possible rather than certain.
examples: M2Q27 (equation certainly has two equal solutions — student selects condition that makes it possible, not certain)
M1Q27 (for which values does the equation allow real solutions — confusion between necessary and sufficient discriminant bound)
M4Q23 (ax=b has one solution — student forgets a≠0 is necessary, not just common)

near_correct_phrasing.visual_similarity_trap: M4Q10 Option A — d1 and d2 have same roots (1 and 2) so student assumes same solution SET, missing that squaring expands the interval.
near_correct_phrasing.special_case_distractors: Answers that are correct for a specific value but wrong in general — M4Q23 assumes a≠0 is always satisfied.

generation_fingerprint:
## ALGEBRA GENERATION FINGERPRINT

Bocconi Algebra tests structural recognition over computation speed. Difficulty stems from logical precision and constraint verification, never from equation length. Due to dataset undercalibration, treat historically Easy-tagged questions as Medium expectations for generation. There are 0 Hard Algebra questions in the dataset — use the Hard blueprint below exclusively for Hard generation.

PROBLEM STRUCTURE AND CALIBRATED UPLIFT:
Medium: Pair algebraic execution with mandatory domain verification. Require computation followed by checking results against a secondary constraint (extracting roots then verifying against linear inequality).
Hard: A parameter must control the number of valid solutions. Hard questions present algebraically correct solutions that must be rejected because they violate a secondary constraint. Wrong answers are engineered as the solutions that pass the first constraint but fail the second.

TRAP AND ANSWER ENGINEERING MODEL:
Extraneous solution filtering is the central difficulty mechanism. Every wrong option must derive from a specific algebraic error.
Correct: Logically airtight intersection of all constraints.
Extraneous Trap: Root from intermediate algebra that fails original domain restriction — the most critical distractor.
Sign Error Trap: Inequality direction reversed on negative division.
Shortcut Trap: General formula applied while ignoring edge case in stem.
Scope Trap: Conclusion true locally but stated universally via qualifier (always, certainly, for all real values).

NECESSARY VS SUFFICIENT CONFUSION (primary ambiguity mechanism):
When stems use certainly, necessarily, or always — students systematically confuse sufficient conditions with necessary ones. A correct answer requires the condition to hold universally. Wrong answers make it possible. Engineer distractors to exploit this: one option is true for a specific case, the correct option is true for all cases.

COGNITIVE FRAMING:
Embed logical qualifiers to shift task from calculation to deduction. Force explicit case splitting for absolute value questions. Use parametric forms (unknown constants a, b, c) to remove integer-testing shortcuts. Algebraic objects should appear visually concise — cognitive load concealed within the interaction of mathematical boundaries, not visible in equation length.

Adhere to the Elimination Mapping Rule: every wrong option must correspond to a specific traceable student error — the generator must be able to state exactly which mistake leads to selecting each distractor.
